evidence: Storage backend behavior is duplicated in parallel Postgres/Turso paths (`load_registry_from_postgres` vs `load_registry_from_turso`, `upsert_loaded_specs_to_postgres` vs `upsert_loaded_specs_to_turso`) in `crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs`.

HTTP handlers directly bind to concrete storage/event internals via `ServerState` (`state.persistent_store()` in `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs`, `state.event_tx.subscribe()` in `crates/temper-server/src/events.rs`) instead of a stable service boundary.

Cross-module contracts are encoded as ad-hoc string literals across modules (`SubmitSpec`, error text matching, status literals, SSE event name), especially in `crates/temper-server/src/insight_generator.rs` and `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs`.

Wrapper fan-out around tenant registration and dispatch adds API surface without adding policy: `register_tenant*`/`try_register_tenant*` in `crates/temper-server/src/registry.rs` and `dispatch_action` -> `dispatch_tenant_action` -> `dispatch_tenant_action_ext` -> `dispatch_tenant_action_core` in `crates/temper-server/src/state/dispatch/actions.rs`.

Cross-cutting authz adaptation is duplicated at multiple call boundaries: BTreeMap/HashMap conversion and decision handling are repeated in `crates/temper-server/src/state/entity_ops.rs` and `crates/temper-server/src/wasm_authz_gate.rs` because `crates/temper-authz/src/engine.rs` exposes a HashMap-shaped interface.

Internal dispatch concern (`cross_entity_booleans`) is exposed in public actor message contract and repeated by callers/tests (`crates/temper-server/src/entity_actor/types.rs`, `crates/temper-server/src/entity_actor/actor.rs`, `crates/temper-platform/tests/system_entity_actors.rs`).

`TursoEventStore` aggregates many unrelated persistence domains (event log, specs, trajectories, evolution records, decisions, constraints, wasm modules/invocations) in one abstraction, increasing blast radius (`crates/temper-store-turso/src/store.rs`; consumers in `crates/temper-cli/src/serve/mod.rs` and `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs`).

System/agent bootstrap flow duplicates parse->verify->register->mark-verified choreography instead of one reusable pipeline (`crates/temper-platform/src/bootstrap.rs`, `crates/temper-cli/src/verify/mod.rs`).

Implicit principal defaults to `System` in `SecurityContext::from_headers` when identity headers are absent (`crates/temper-authz/src/context.rs:51-54`), and `authorize_or_bypass` unconditionally allows `System` (`crates/temper-authz/src/engine.rs:212-227`).

Endpoints in `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs` that mutate state (`handle_sentinel_check`, `handle_feature_requests`) perform writes/dispatches without any Cedar check (`:167-185`, `:221-269`).

Tenant resolution falls back to first registered tenant or default when `x-tenant-id` is missing (`crates/temper-server/src/odata/common.rs:13-37`), while read endpoints enumerate across all tenants (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/specs.rs:20-66`).

Cross-tenant record/insight listing endpoints (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs:17-128`) expose data without tenant/principal scoping, while a sibling endpoint in the same module family applies Cedar checks (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs:303-327`).

findings.summary: Postgres and Turso integration paths are duplicated, creating backend drift risk across modules.
evidence: `load_registry_from_postgres` and `load_registry_from_turso` repeat the same tenant grouping, CSDL parse, registry registration, and status restoration flow (`storage.rs` lines ~212-369).
`upsert_loaded_specs_to_postgres` and `upsert_loaded_specs_to_turso` duplicate the same spec/constraint sync policy (`storage.rs` lines ~77-128 and ~472-499).
`records_list.rs` bypasses any shared backend-neutral read path and calls Turso-specific methods directly (`state.persistent_store().list_*`), reinforcing split backend behavior.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a backend-neutral persistence interface (e.g., `SpecPersistence`/`EvolutionReadStore`) and move shared restore/upsert orchestration into one implementation path; keep backend-specific code as thin adapters. Route `records_list` through that same interface instead of direct Turso calls.

findings.summary: Cross-module behavior depends on magic strings rather than typed contracts.
evidence: `insight_generator.rs` derives behavior from literal action/error text (`SubmitSpec`, `EntitySetNotFound`, `Authorization denied`, `ActionNotFound`) instead of typed event/error categories.
`records_list.rs` accepts raw status/type strings in query params and composes JSON using string keys directly, with no shared enum/schema boundary.
`events.rs` emits a literal SSE event name (`"state_change"`) with no shared protocol constant across publishers/consumers.
findings.suggestion: Define shared protocol types/constants in a common domain module (action kinds, error categories, record status/type enums, SSE event names) and update handlers/generators to consume typed values instead of string matching.

findings.summary: ServerState exposes low-level internals across modules, increasing coupling and blast radius.
evidence: `events.rs` accesses channel internals directly via `state.event_tx.subscribe()`.
`records_list.rs` accesses persistence internals via `state.persistent_store()` and performs response shaping in the handler layer.
`dispatch_blocking.rs` adds another `ServerState` method that forwards a wide request bag into internal dispatch, reinforcing `ServerState` as an orchestration hub rather than a narrow boundary.
findings.suggestion: Refactor `ServerState` consumers to depend on focused service interfaces (event feed, evolution read API, integration dispatch API). Keep `ServerState` as composition/wiring only, with handlers bound to trait-backed services.

findings.summary: Compatibility wrappers create deep call chains with little added behavior.
evidence: `SpecRegistry` exposes multiple near-identical entrypoints (`register_tenant`, `try_register_tenant`, `register_tenant_with_reactions`, `register_tenant_with_reactions_and_constraints`, and corresponding `try_*`) that mostly forward to `try_register_tenant_with_reactions_and_constraints`.
`dispatch_action` -> `dispatch_tenant_action` -> `dispatch_tenant_action_ext` -> `dispatch_tenant_action_core` adds forwarding layers where only defaults are injected.

Entity and OData paths keep parallel legacy/single-tenant fallbacks (`get_or_spawn_actor`/default tenant wrapper; `resolve_entity_type` and CSDL/entity set fallback to legacy maps), which leaks migration-era branching into hot paths.
findings.suggestion: Collapse wrapper families into one canonical API per concern (registration and dispatch), keep tiny compatibility shims only at external boundaries, and isolate legacy fallback in a dedicated compatibility adapter/module rather than in every operational method.

findings.summary: Authz request-shape adaptation is duplicated across server and WASM paths.
evidence: `authorize` and `authorize_with_context` in `entity_ops.rs` both clone-convert BTreeMap attrs to HashMap and duplicate decision telemetry plumbing.
`wasm_authz_gate.rs` rebuilds similar conversion logic (`BTreeMap` to `HashMap`) and context assembly before calling `authorize_or_bypass`.
`AuthzEngine::authorize` requires `HashMap<String, serde_json::Value>`, forcing callers to perform per-call shape adaptation instead of sharing one adapter abstraction.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single authz request adapter (e.g., `AuthzRequest` plus `From` impls for server/wasm contexts) and move conversion/telemetry into one shared path; relax engine input to accept iterator/map-like traits where possible.

findings.summary: Entity actor API leaks dispatch-layer cross-entity guard internals to callers.
evidence: `EntityMsg::Action` requires `cross_entity_booleans: BTreeMap<String, bool>` in its public message shape.
Many call sites/tests repeatedly pass `std::collections::BTreeMap::new()` because they do not own cross-entity resolution concerns.
Guard-key encoding format (`__xref:{type}:{field}`) is implied by effect/guard internals, making actor API less honest and harder to use correctly.
findings.suggestion: Hide cross-entity guard inputs behind actor-internal resolution or an optional/internal envelope with defaults so normal callers send only semantic action data (`name`, `params`).

findings.summary: Single Turso store abstraction owns too many unrelated persistence domains.
evidence: `TursoEventStore` contains event-store trait behavior plus spec storage, trajectory analytics, feature requests, evolution records, decisions, tenant policies/constraints, and wasm module/invocation persistence in one type.
Operational modules (`serve` and evolution handlers) depend directly on this broad surface, increasing coupling to one persistence hub.
Schema migration and runtime query responsibilities for multiple domains are co-located in one file, so unrelated changes share blast radius.
findings.suggestion: Split Turso persistence into domain-focused repositories (events/specs/evolution/wasm/policy) behind narrower traits and compose them in startup wiring; keep `EventStore` implementation thin and domain-agnostic.

findings.summary: Spec parse/verify/register workflow is duplicated across bootstrap and CLI flows.
evidence: `bootstrap_system_tenant` and `bootstrap_agent_specs` repeat the same four-stage orchestration: parse all specs, run verification cascade, register tenant specs, then mark verification statuses.
`verify::run` and serve-time loading also implement overlapping verification/loading choreography separately, with different defaults and reporting behavior.
No shared abstraction defines one canonical spec onboarding pipeline, so behavior drift risk increases as rules evolve.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared `SpecOnboardingPipeline` (load -> parse -> verify -> register -> annotate status) used by platform bootstrap, `temper verify`, and serve-time app loading with configurable policy knobs.

findings.summary: Evolution read APIs are Turso-bound while server startup and docs advertise dual backend support.
evidence: README describes persistent state/event sourcing on Postgres or Turso/libSQL as a core platform capability (README.md:41-43,216-220).
Postgres startup path explicitly migrates and wires `PostgresRecordStore` (`crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs`:39-58).

Observe evolution endpoints only call `state.persistent_store()` (Turso path) and otherwise return empty results instead of querying Postgres (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs`:23-27,72-80,88-90,123-127).
findings.suggestion: Introduce a backend-agnostic evolution repository interface in `temper-server` and implement both Turso and Postgres adapters; make `records_list` resolve through that interface so read behavior matches the advertised storage contract.

findings.summary: `temper init` scaffolds evolution filesystem hierarchy that runtime paths do not use.
evidence: `temper init` always creates `evolution/observations|problems|analyses|decisions|insights` directories (`crates/temper-cli/src/init/mod.rs`:59-64).
Server persistence and restore paths are database-backed (`crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs`:77-127,212-294,297-369,472-499).
Evolution listing handlers read from Turso store and provide no file-backed branch (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs`:23-27,88-90).
findings.suggestion: Either remove the unused evolution directory scaffolding from `init` or add a clearly bounded file-backed adapter and selection policy; avoid presenting filesystem structure as a runtime contract when persistence is DB-only.

findings.summary: Evolution/inference logic relies on ad hoc string protocols across modules instead of shared typed contracts.
evidence: Resolution logic is keyed on `entry.action == "SubmitSpec"` and substring matching of free-form error text (`crates/temper-server/src/insight_generator.rs`:83-85,283-285,431-446).

Records API counts by hard-coded record-type strings (`"Observation"`, `"Problem"`, etc.) while query params are documented as lowercase literals (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs`:11-14,56-64).
README positions the evolution chain as a first-class governance mechanism, increasing risk when semantics are encoded as fragile strings rather than shared domain types (README.md:154-155,219-224).
findings.suggestion: Define shared enums/constants for trajectory actions, error categories, and evolution record types in a common crate, and make ingestion, insight generation, and observe handlers consume those types instead of string literals/substrings.

findings.summary: Missing identity headers default to `System`, turning auth checks into allow-by-default
evidence: `SecurityContext::from_headers` initializes `kind` as `PrincipalKind::System` (`context.rs:53`) when no `x-temper-principal-kind` header exists.
`AuthzEngine::authorize_or_bypass` returns `Allow` for any `System` principal (`engine.rs:225-227`).
`handle_update_feature_request` builds context from headers and relies on Cedar authorization (`operations.rs:315-323`), so missing headers still pass via System bypass.
findings.suggestion: Change default request principal to a non-bypass identity (for example `Anonymous`/`Agent`), reserve `System` only for explicitly internal call paths, and require explicit trusted marker for any bypass path. Then update endpoint auth helpers to reject missing/invalid principal headers for external routes.

findings.summary: State-mutating evolution handlers run without authorization checks
evidence: `handle_sentinel_check` triggers alert/insight persistence and entity creation via `persist_alerts`/`persist_insights` with no principal/auth check (`operations.rs:167-185`).
`handle_feature_requests` (GET) upserts feature requests and dispatches `CreateFeatureRequest` actions (`operations.rs:221-269`) without Cedar authorization.
Documentation claims every state-changing action is authorized (`README.md:25`, `README.md:35`, `README.md:70`), but these mutating handlers bypass that model.
findings.suggestion: Apply a shared authorization middleware/policy gate for all mutation-capable handlers (including GET handlers with side effects), and split read endpoints from write/refresh behavior so non-mutating reads cannot perform writes implicitly.

findings.summary: Tenant is inferred from server state when header is missing, enabling cross-tenant drift
evidence: `extract_tenant` silently falls back to first registered tenant, then default tenant (`odata/common.rs:23-37`) instead of rejecting missing tenant identity.
`list_specs` iterates all tenants and returns full per-tenant spec metadata (`observe/specs.rs:25-66`).
`list_evolution_records` and `list_evolution_insights` return global Turso rows without tenant filter (`records_list.rs:24-64`, `:89-115`).
findings.suggestion: Require explicit tenant binding for external requests (header/token claim) and reject absent tenant context; for intentionally cross-tenant endpoints, enforce explicit privileged permission and audit marker rather than implicit fallback behavior.
